name: Technical Architecture Strategy
consequences: Adopting open standards for fallback authentication reduces reliance on platform-specific signals but risks compatibility challenges with existing MitID infrastructure. This could delay adoption if legacy systems require extensive reconfiguration.
options: Implement FIDO2/WebAuthn-based authentication with GrapheneOS as a privacy-respecting alternative to mainstream OSes
Develop a hybrid model integrating platform-neutral tokens with existing MitID trust chains
Pilot decentralized identity protocols using blockchain-based verifiable credentials for critical services
review: This lever governs the tension between innovation and compatibility, but the options overlook the complexity of integrating decentralized systems with Denmark's existing eIDAS compliance framework.

name: Policy Framing for Resilience
consequences: Reframing dependency as a continuity-of-service risk shifts focus from ideological concerns to operational necessity. However, this approach may face pushback from stakeholders prioritizing short-term convenience over long-term stability.
options: Advocate for mandatory platform-neutral access requirements in public-sector procurement contracts
Position fallback authentication as a critical infrastructure resilience measure under national cybersecurity policies
Leverage EU Digital Identity Wallet guidelines to mandate interoperability with non-platform-specific solutions
review: This lever addresses the tension between operational risk and political feasibility, but the options miss opportunities to directly engage with local government IT departments' maintenance priorities.

name: Coalition Engagement Models
consequences: Building cross-sector coalitions strengthens advocacy but requires significant time and resource investment. Success depends on aligning diverse interests without diluting the project's technical and policy objectives.
options: Form a public-private partnership with Nordic fintech firms to co-develop platform-neutral authentication pilots
Create a technical advisory board including academic researchers and open-source security experts
Engage civil society organizations to frame dependency as a democratic accountability issue
review: This lever balances stakeholder inclusion against project focus, but the options underemphasize strategies for managing conflicting priorities within the coalition.

name: Procurement Influence Tactics
consequences: Influencing AltID procurement requirements could institutionalize platform neutrality but faces resistance from vendors invested in existing ecosystems. Success depends on aligning with broader EU digital sovereignty initiatives.
options: Embed platform-neutral authentication criteria in AltID's technical specifications through formal feedback mechanisms
Use phased procurement milestones to incentivize incremental adoption of fallback capabilities
Collaborate with EU member states to create joint procurement standards for digital identity solutions
review: This lever navigates the tension between regulatory influence and vendor dynamics, but the options fail to address potential legal challenges from incumbent platform providers.

name: Standards Alignment Approaches
consequences: Aligning with eIDAS2 and NSIS standards ensures regulatory compliance but may limit innovation in fallback authentication models. This could create friction with more progressive EU identity initiatives.
options: Develop certification pathways for platform-neutral authentication methods under existing eIDAS2 frameworks
Propose amendments to NSIS requirements to explicitly include fallback authentication capabilities
Participate in EU technical working groups to shape Digital Identity Wallet specifications for interoperability
review: This lever balances compliance with innovation, but the options overlook the risk of standards becoming tools to maintain status quo vendor dominance.

name: Stakeholder Influence Dynamics
consequences: Shifting engagement strategies toward non-traditional allies could accelerate policy momentum but risks alienating core institutional partners. Prioritizing civil society coalitions may amplify public pressure but could dilute technical credibility among regulators.
options: Forge alliances with Nordic digital sovereignty advocacy groups to co-author policy briefs
Leverage academic institutions to conduct third-party audits of platform dependency risks
Establish parallel technical working groups with non-state identity providers to demonstrate alternatives
review: This lever governs the tension between institutional legitimacy and grassroots mobilization, but the options miss the risk of fragmenting stakeholder attention across multiple parallel initiatives.

name: Regulatory Pressure Mechanisms
consequences: Focusing on EU-level regulatory engagement could create cross-border momentum but may delay national implementation. Targeting specific procurement clauses might force immediate changes but could provoke retaliatory lobbying from platform providers.
options: Propose amendments to eIDAS2 requirements through formal technical working groups
Initiate targeted inquiries with the Danish Competition Authority about supplier concentration risks
Develop model procurement language for fallback authentication pathways to share with public-sector buyers
review: This lever addresses the tension between national and supranational governance, but the options overlook the complexity of aligning Danish requirements with EU-wide technical standards.

name: Technical Demonstration Prioritization
consequences: Focusing on hardware token demonstrators may strengthen security perceptions but could limit adoption due to cost barriers. Browser-based approaches might increase accessibility but risk being dismissed as insufficiently robust.
options: Build a minimal viable fallback authentication prototype using open-source FIDO2 standards
Develop a cross-platform identity verification tool compatible with both Android and iOS
Create a simulated emergency authentication scenario for public-sector continuity planning
review: This lever balances technical innovation with practical adoption, but the options fail to address the challenge of demonstrating value without full production integration.

name: International Standards Advocacy
consequences: Aligning with EU Digital Identity Wallet frameworks could provide institutional backing but might constrain national flexibility. Focusing on global interoperability standards could broaden impact but requires significant technical adaptation.
options: Participate in European Commission technical advisory groups for digital identity frameworks
Publish open-source reference implementations aligned with W3C WebAuthn standards
Collaborate with Nordic counterparts to develop shared technical requirements for identity systems
review: This lever navigates the tension between regional and global influence, but the options underestimate the time required to shift established international standardization processes.

name: Public-Private Collaboration Models
consequences: Engaging with private sector identity providers could accelerate adoption but risks compromising independence. Focusing on niche providers might limit scale but could create more agile partnership opportunities.
options: Establish a joint research initiative with non-platform mobile OS developers
Create a certification pathway for third-party authentication services compatible with AltID
Develop a shared risk-mitigation framework with financial sector identity stakeholders
review: This lever addresses the tension between independence and collaboration, but the options miss the challenge of maintaining neutrality while engaging with commercial entities.

name: Contingency Planning Integration
consequences: Embedding fallback requirements into existing continuity plans could ensure immediate adoption but might lead to superficial compliance. Focusing on high-risk sectors could demonstrate value but may exclude broader systemic changes.
options: Integrate fallback authentication requirements into national cybersecurity incident response protocols
Develop sector-specific contingency plans for public administration identity systems
Create a simulated crisis scenario testing platform-neutral authentication pathways
review: This lever balances immediate preparedness with long-term transformation, but the options overlook the need for sustained political commitment beyond initial implementation.

name: Interoperability Standards Advocacy
consequences: This lever would push for open standards to reduce reliance on proprietary systems, potentially weakening incumbent vendor influence. However, it may face resistance from established platforms, leading to prolonged negotiations and delayed implementation.
options: Promote open-source authentication protocols to reduce vendor dependency
Advocate for eIDAS2 compliance with mandatory fallback mechanisms
Develop cross-platform authentication frameworks through EU technical working groups
review: Governs open standards vs. vendor interests, but the options overlook legal barriers from existing contracts.

name: Fallback Infrastructure Design
consequences: This lever would establish alternative authentication paths, enhancing resilience. However, it could divert resources from immediate platform integration efforts, risking short-term service disruptions.
options: Design hardware-token-based authentication systems with minimal OS dependencies
Create browser-based fallback mechanisms using decentralized identity protocols
Develop a platform-neutral identity verification layer integrated with existing eIDAS frameworks
review: Balances resilience with operational continuity, but the options neglect potential user adoption challenges.

name: Resilience-Driven Policy Integration
consequences: This lever would institutionalize resilience considerations, but may encounter pushback from policymakers prioritizing cost over long-term stability.
options: Frame digital identity reforms as critical infrastructure resilience imperatives
Embed fallback requirements in national procurement policies for public services
Lobby for mandatory resilience audits of all digital identity service providers
review: Focuses on policy resilience vs. fiscal constraints, but the options ignore technical feasibility of audit requirements.

name: Technical Evidence Mobilization
consequences: This lever would generate credible technical evidence, but could be perceived as biased if not independently verified, undermining trust.
options: Publish open-source demonstrators to validate platform-neutral authentication models
Conduct third-party security audits to build credibility for alternative systems
Release white papers on supplier concentration risks to influence stakeholder perceptions
review: Balances technical proof with credibility, but the options fail to address potential misinformation campaigns by incumbents.

name: Institutional Alignment Strategies
consequences: This lever would strengthen institutional support, but may lead to bureaucratic delays and conflicting priorities among partners.
options: Forge partnerships with EU digital identity working groups to align national policies
Establish joint task forces with public-sector stakeholders to co-design fallback solutions
Engage with Danish universities to create research centers focused on platform-neutral identity
review: Balances collaboration with autonomy, but the options neglect resource allocation conflicts.
